```{r}
    values = c("0" = "deepskyblue2", "1" = "red"),
    labels = c("0" = "AAER = 0", "1" = "AAER = 1")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Density Plot of DEBT_TO_EQUITY by AAER",
    x = "DEBT_TO_EQUITY",
    y = "Density",
    fill = "AAER"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Data Modelling Setup

```{r load-data}
dt <- copy(df_model_balanced)
dt[, .N, AAER]

set.seed(42)
```

## Feature Selection and Data Cleaning

This step prepares the dataset for model training by defining AAER as the target variable and selecting relevant predictors while removing identifiers and non-informative columns using setdiff().

A modeling dataset is created with only the selected features and target. Observations with missing values are removed, and any infinite values are converted to NA and subsequently dropped.

The final dataset is fully cleaned and suitable for machine learning, with a summary of total observations and fraud cases reported for validation.

```{r feature-selection}
target <- "AAER"

#setdiff function is to remove unwanted columns for training
features <- setdiff(
  names(dt),
  c("id", "V1", "code", "year", "COMPANY NAME (6001)", target)
)

# Keep only complete cases
model_dt <- dt[, .SD, .SDcols = c(features, target)]
model_dt <- model_dt[complete.cases(model_dt)]

# Replace Inf / -Inf with NA then drop
model_dt[,
  (features) := lapply(.SD, function(x) ifelse(is.infinite(x), NA, x)),
  .SDcols = features
]
model_dt <- model_dt[complete.cases(model_dt)]

cat(sprintf(
  "Final modelling dataset: %d rows, %d fraud cases (%.1f%%)\n",
  nrow(model_dt),
  sum(model_dt$AAER),
  100 * mean(model_dt$AAER)
))
```

## Stratified Train / Test Split (80/20)

This step first performs a stratified 80/20 split so that the fraud proportion is preserved in both the training and test sets.

```{r train-test-split}
model_df <- as.data.frame(model_dt)
model_df$AAER <- as.factor(model_df$AAER)
levels(model_df$AAER) <- c("NonFraud", "Fraud")

train_idx <- createDataPartition(model_df$AAER, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
train_raw <- model_df[train_idx, ]
test <- model_df[-train_idx, ]

cat(sprintf("Train: %d rows | Test: %d rows\n", nrow(train_raw), nrow(test)))
cat(sprintf(
  "Train fraud rate: %.1f%% | Test fraud rate: %.1f%%\n",
  100 * mean(train_raw$AAER == "Fraud"),
  100 * mean(test$AAER == "Fraud")
))
```

## Sampling Strategy Comparison

This section evaluates three sampling strategies: standard, oversampling (ROSE), and undersampling, to address class imbalance in fraud detection. Logistic Regression is used as a benchmark model, and performance is assessed on a held-out test set to determine which approach generalises best .

### Produce Training Sets

Three versions of the training dataset are created:

-   Standard: original data without modification
-   Oversampling (ROSE): generates synthetic fraud observations to balance classes
-   Undersampling: reduces non-fraud observations to match the number of fraud cases

The size and fraud proportion of each dataset are reported to show how class balance changes.

```{r sampling-sets}
# Standard (no resampling)
train_standard <- train_raw

# Oversampling — ROSE synthesises new minority observations
train_over <- ROSE(AAER ~ ., data = train_raw, seed = 42)$data

# Undersampling — randomly drop majority class down to fraud count
n_fraud <- sum(train_raw$AAER == "Fraud")
maj_idx <- which(train_raw$AAER == "NonFraud")
keep_maj <- sample(maj_idx, size = n_fraud, replace = FALSE)
train_under <- train_raw[c(which(train_raw$AAER == "Fraud"), keep_maj), ]

cat("\n===== SAMPLING STRATEGY SUMMARY =====\n")
cat(sprintf("  Standard  : %d rows | Fraud: %.1f%%\n",
            nrow(train_standard), 100 * mean(train_standard$AAER == "Fraud")))
cat(sprintf("  Oversample: %d rows | Fraud: %.1f%%\n",
            nrow(train_over), 100 * mean(train_over$AAER == "Fraud")))
cat(sprintf("  Undersample:%d rows | Fraud: %.1f%%\n",
            nrow(train_under), 100 * mean(train_under$AAER == "Fraud")))
```

### Run Comparison

Each training dataset is used to train a Logistic Regression model with 5-fold cross-validation. The trained model is then evaluated on the same held-out test set. Performance metrics include: - AUC-ROC - PR-AUC - F1-score - Precision and Recall

This ensures a fair comparison of how each sampling strategy performs on real, unseen data.

```{r sampling-comparison}
ctrl_compare <- trainControl(
  method = "cv",
  number = 5,
  classProbs = TRUE,
  summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
  savePredictions = "final"
)

compare_sampling <- function(train_df, label, X_test, y_test) {
  X_tr <- train_df[, features]
  y_tr <- factor(train_df$AAER, levels = c("NonFraud", "Fraud"))

  fit <- train(
    x = X_tr,
    y = y_tr,
    method = "glm",
    family = "binomial",
    metric = "ROC",
    trControl = ctrl_compare
  )

  probs <- predict(fit, newdata = X_test, type = "prob")[, "Fraud"]
  preds <- predict(fit, newdata = X_test)

  roc_obj <- roc(y_test, probs, levels = c("NonFraud", "Fraud"), quiet = TRUE)
  auc_roc <- as.numeric(auc(roc_obj))

  pr_obj <- pr.curve(
    scores.class0 = probs[y_test == "Fraud"],
    scores.class1 = probs[y_test == "NonFraud"],
    curve = TRUE
  )
  auc_pr <- pr_obj$auc.integral

  cm <- confusionMatrix(preds, y_test, positive = "Fraud")
  precision <- cm$byClass["Precision"]
  recall <- cm$byClass["Recall"]
  f1 <- cm$byClass["F1"]

  cat(sprintf("\n  [%s]\n", label))
  cat(sprintf("    AUC-ROC : %.4f\n", auc_roc))
  cat(sprintf("    PR-AUC  : %.4f\n", auc_pr))
  cat(sprintf("    F1      : %.4f\n", f1))
  cat(sprintf("    Precision: %.4f | Recall: %.4f\n", precision, recall))

  list(
    label = label, auc_roc = auc_roc, auc_pr = auc_pr,
    f1 = f1, precision = precision, recall = recall,
    roc_obj = roc_obj, pr_obj = pr_obj
  )
}

X_test_cmp <- test[, features]
y_test_cmp <- test$AAER

cat("\n--- Sampling Comparison (Logistic Regression on held-out test set) ---\n")
samp_results <- list(
  standard = compare_sampling(train_standard, "Standard (no resampling)", X_test_cmp, y_test_cmp),
  over     = compare_sampling(train_over,     "Oversampling (ROSE)",      X_test_cmp, y_test_cmp),
  under    = compare_sampling(train_under,    "Undersampling",            X_test_cmp, y_test_cmp)
)
```

### Comparison Table

The results from all sampling strategies are combined into a summary table showing key metrics. This allows for direct comparison of model performance across different sampling approaches.

```{r sampling-table}
samp_tbl <- rbindlist(lapply(samp_results, function(r) {
  data.table(
    Strategy  = r$label,
    AUC_ROC   = round(r$auc_roc, 4),
    PR_AUC    = round(r$auc_pr, 4),
    F1        = round(r$f1, 4),
    Precision = round(r$precision, 4),
    Recall    = round(r$recall, 4)
  )
}))
print(samp_tbl)
```

### Comparison Plots

```{r sampling-roc, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
plot(samp_results$standard$roc_obj, col = "#377EB8", lwd = 2,
     main = "Sampling Strategy Comparison — ROC Curves (LR)")
plot(samp_results$over$roc_obj,  col = "#4DAF4A", lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
plot(samp_results$under$roc_obj, col = "#E41A1C", lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = c(
         sprintf("Standard    (%.3f)", samp_results$standard$auc_roc),
         sprintf("Oversample  (%.3f)", samp_results$over$auc_roc),
         sprintf("Undersample (%.3f)", samp_results$under$auc_roc)
       ),
       col = c("#377EB8", "#4DAF4A", "#E41A1C"), lwd = 2)
```

```{r sampling-pr, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
plot(samp_results$standard$pr_obj, col = "#377EB8", lwd = 2,
     main = "Sampling Strategy Comparison — PR Curves (LR)", auc.main = FALSE)
plot(samp_results$over$pr_obj,  col = "#4DAF4A", lwd = 2, add = TRUE, auc.main = FALSE)
plot(samp_results$under$pr_obj, col = "#E41A1C", lwd = 2, add = TRUE, auc.main = FALSE)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(
         sprintf("Standard    (%.3f)", samp_results$standard$auc_pr),
         sprintf("Oversample  (%.3f)", samp_results$over$auc_pr),
         sprintf("Undersample (%.3f)", samp_results$under$auc_pr)
       ),
       col = c("#377EB8", "#4DAF4A", "#E41A1C"), lwd = 2)
```

```{r sampling-bar, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
samp_plot_df <- melt(samp_tbl, id.vars = "Strategy",
                     measure.vars = c("AUC_ROC", "PR_AUC", "F1"),
                     variable.name = "Metric", value.name = "Score")
ggplot(samp_plot_df, aes(x = Strategy, y = Score, fill = Metric)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "AUC_ROC" = "#1F3A5F",   
    "PR_AUC"  = "#4F8A8B",   
    "F1"      = "#FFE600"    
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Sampling Strategy Comparison (Logistic Regression)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Score",
    fill = "Metric"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5)
  )
```

### Select Winner

The best sampling strategy is selected using the ranking rule:

Score = PR-AUC + 0.5 × F1

PR-AUC is prioritised because fraud detection is an imbalanced problem, and PR-AUC better reflects performance on the minority class. F1 is included to balance precision and recall.

Based on this rule: Oversampling (ROSE) achieved the highest score It provides the best overall balance between detecting fraud (recall) and maintaining precision

Therefore, ROSE is selected as the final sampling strategy, and the model will be trained on this dataset going forward.

```{r sampling-winner}
samp_tbl[, rank_score := PR_AUC + 0.5 * F1]
winner_label <- samp_tbl[
  which.max(rank_score), Strategy
]
cat(sprintf("\n>> Winning sampling strategy: %s <<\n", winner_label))

#train_best <- train_standard
train_best <- switch(winner_label,
  "Standard (no resampling)" = train_standard,
  "Oversampling (ROSE)"       = train_over,
  "Undersampling"             = train_under,
  train_over   # fallback
)

cat(sprintf(
  "   Main pipeline will train on %d rows (Fraud: %.1f%%)\n",
  nrow(train_best),
  100 * mean(train_best$AAER == "Fraud")
))
```

## Model Training

This section trains all candidate models using the selected sampling strategy from the earlier comparison. The same training and test variables are used throughout, and all models share the same 5-fold cross-validation settings so that model performance can be compared fairly. The factor levels are ordered as NonFraud and Fraud to ensure that fraud is treated as the positive class in evaluation.

```{r training-setup}
X_train <- train_best[, features]
y_train <- factor(train_best$AAER, levels = c("NonFraud", "Fraud"))
X_test  <- test[, features]
y_test  <- factor(test$AAER, levels = c("NonFraud", "Fraud"))

ctrl <- trainControl(
  method = "cv",
  number = 5,
  classProbs = TRUE,
  summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
  savePredictions = "final"
)
```

### Logistic Regression

This section trains all candidate models using the selected sampling strategy from the earlier comparison. The same training and test variables are used throughout, and all models share the same 5-fold cross-validation settings so that model performance can be compared fairly. The factor levels are ordered as NonFraud and Fraud to ensure that fraud is treated as the positive class in evaluation.

```{r train-lr}
fit_lr <- train(
  x = X_train,
  y = y_train,
  method = "glm",
  family = "binomial",
  metric = "ROC",
  trControl = ctrl
)
cat("\nTraining Logistic Regression completed\n")
```

### LASSO

This code trains a LASSO Logistic Regression model using glmnet. LASSO applies an L1 penalty that shrinks less important coefficients toward zero, which helps reduce overfitting and perform automatic feature selection. The tuning parameter lambda is selected by cross-validation, and the retained predictors are reported.

```{r train-lasso}

lasso_grid <- expand.grid(
  alpha  = 1,
  lambda = 10^seq(-4, 0, length.out = 50)
)
fit_lasso <- train(
  x = as.matrix(X_train),
  y = y_train,
  method = "glmnet",
  family = "binomial",
  metric = "ROC",
  trControl = ctrl,
  tuneGrid = lasso_grid
)
cat(sprintf("  Best lambda: %.6f\n", fit_lasso$bestTune$lambda))

lasso_coef <- coef(fit_lasso$finalModel, s = fit_lasso$bestTune$lambda)
lasso_retained <- rownames(lasso_coef)[which(abs(lasso_coef) > 0)]
cat(sprintf(
  "  Features retained by LASSO: %s\n",
  paste(lasso_retained[lasso_retained != "(Intercept)"], collapse = ", ")
))
cat("Training LASSO completed\n")
```

### Decision Tree (Pruned)

This code trains a Decision Tree model and tunes the complexity parameter (cp) to obtain a pruned tree. Pruning reduces unnecessary splits and helps improve performance on unseen data by limiting overfitting.

```{r train-dt}

fit_dt <- train(
  x = X_train,
  y = y_train,
  method = "rpart",
  metric = "ROC",
  trControl = ctrl,
  tuneLength = 15
)
cat(sprintf("  Best cp: %.5f\n", fit_dt$bestTune$cp))
cat("Training Pruned Decision completed\n")
```

### Random Forest

This code trains a Random Forest model using 500 decision trees (ntree = 500) and tunes the mtry parameter (number of variables randomly sampled at each split) across 5 candidate values (tuneLength = 5). The ensemble approach reduces overfitting compared to a single decision tree by averaging predictions across many trees trained on bootstrapped samples.

```{r train-rf}
fit_rf <- train(
  x = X_train,
  y = y_train,
  method = "rf",
  metric = "ROC",
  trControl = ctrl,
  tuneLength = 5,
  ntree = 500
)

cat("Training Random Forest completed\n")
```

### XGBoost

This code trains an XGBoost model by performing an exhaustive grid search over 7 hyperparameters: number of boosting rounds (nrounds), tree depth (max_depth), learning rate (eta), minimum loss reduction for a split (gamma), fraction of features sampled per tree (colsample_bytree), minimum child node weight (min_child_weight), and row subsampling ratio (subsample). Each combination is evaluated using ROC-AUC, and the best-performing configuration is printed via bestTune. This tuning process helps balance model complexity and generalisation performance.

```{r}
# Parameter adjustment
xgb_grid <- expand.grid(
  nrounds          = c(100, 200),
  max_depth        = c(3, 6),
  eta              = c(0.05, 0.1),
  gamma            = c(0, 0.1),
  colsample_bytree = c(0.7, 0.8),
  min_child_weight = c(1, 3),
  subsample        = c(0.7, 0.8)
)

fit_xgb <- train(
  x = X_train,
  y = y_train,
  method = "xgbTree",
  metric = "ROC",
  trControl = ctrl,
  tuneGrid = xgb_grid,
  verbose = 0,
  verbosity = 0 # Removes all the generated warning messages
)

print(fit_xgb$bestTune)
cat("Training XGBoost completed\n")
```

## Evaluation

### Evaluation Function

```{r eval-function}
# use_matrix = TRUE for LASSO (glmnet requires matrix input for predict)
evaluate_model <- function(model, X_test, y_test, model_name, use_matrix = FALSE) {
  X_input <- if (use_matrix) as.matrix(X_test) else X_test

  probs <- predict(model, newdata = X_input, type = "prob")[, "Fraud"]
  preds <- predict(model, newdata = X_input)

  roc_obj <- roc(y_test, probs, levels = c("NonFraud", "Fraud"), quiet = TRUE)
  auc_roc <- as.numeric(auc(roc_obj))

  pr_obj <- pr.curve(
    scores.class0 = probs[y_test == "Fraud"],
    scores.class1 = probs[y_test == "NonFraud"],
    curve = TRUE
  )
  auc_pr <- pr_obj$auc.integral

  cm        <- confusionMatrix(preds, y_test, positive = "Fraud")
  precision <- cm$byClass["Precision"]
  recall    <- cm$byClass["Recall"]
  f1        <- cm$byClass["F1"]

  cat(sprintf("\n--- %s ---\n", model_name))
  cat(sprintf("  AUC-ROC : %.4f\n", auc_roc))
  cat(sprintf("  PR-AUC  : %.4f\n", auc_pr))
  cat(sprintf("  F1      : %.4f\n", f1))
  cat(sprintf("  Precision: %.4f | Recall: %.4f\n", precision, recall))

  list(
    model_name = model_name,
    auc_roc = auc_roc, auc_pr = auc_pr, f1 = f1,
    precision = precision, recall = recall,
    probs = probs, preds = preds,
    roc_obj = roc_obj, pr_obj = pr_obj
  )
}
```

### Run Evaluation

```{r run-evaluation}
results_train <- list(
  LR    = evaluate_model(fit_lr,    X_train, y_train, "Logistic Regression"),
  LASSO = evaluate_model(fit_lasso, X_train, y_train, "LASSO", use_matrix = TRUE),
  DT    = evaluate_model(fit_dt,    X_train, y_train, "Decision Tree (Pruned)"),
  RF    = evaluate_model(fit_rf,    X_train, y_train, "Random Forest"),
  XGB   = evaluate_model(fit_xgb,   X_train, y_train, "XGBoost")
)

results <- list(
  LR    = evaluate_model(fit_lr,    X_test, y_test, "Logistic Regression"),
  LASSO = evaluate_model(fit_lasso, X_test, y_test, "LASSO", use_matrix = TRUE),
  DT    = evaluate_model(fit_dt,    X_test, y_test, "Decision Tree (Pruned)"),
  RF    = evaluate_model(fit_rf,    X_test, y_test, "Random Forest"),
  XGB   = evaluate_model(fit_xgb,   X_test, y_test, "XGBoost")
)
```

### Summary Tables

```{r summary-tables}
summary_tbl_train <- rbindlist(lapply(results_train, function(r) {
  data.table(
    Model     = r$model_name,
    AUC_ROC   = round(r$auc_roc, 4),
    PR_AUC    = round(r$auc_pr, 4),
    F1        = round(r$f1, 4),
    Precision = round(r$precision, 4),
    Recall    = round(r$recall, 4)
  )
}))

summary_tbl <- rbindlist(lapply(results, function(r) {
  data.table(
    Model     = r$model_name,
    AUC_ROC   = round(r$auc_roc, 4),
    PR_AUC    = round(r$auc_pr, 4),
    F1        = round(r$f1, 4),
    Precision = round(r$precision, 4),
    Recall    = round(r$recall, 4)
  )
}))

cat("\n===== TRAIN SET PERFORMANCE =====\n")
print(summary_tbl_train)
cat("\n===== TEST SET PERFORMANCE =====\n")
print(summary_tbl)
```

Across the test set, all models exhibit very similar performance, with only marginal differences observed across AUC, PR-AUC, F1-score, precision, and recall. This indicates that no single model provides a clear overall predictive advantage, and the models can be considered broadly comparable in their ability to distinguish between fraudulent and non-fraudulent firms. Notably, more complex models such as Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost show near-perfect performance on the training set but substantially lower performance on the test set, suggesting the presence of overfitting, where the models capture noise rather than generalisable patterns.

Given the objective of predicting fraud (AAER = 1), recall is prioritised as the primary evaluation metric, as it directly reflects the model’s ability to correctly identify fraudulent firms. In this context, false negatives are significantly more costly than false positives, since failing to detect a fraudulent firm poses greater risk than incorrectly flagging a non-fraudulent one. Therefore, model selection is guided by maximising recall while ensuring that other metrics, particularly precision and F1-score, remain at acceptable levels.

Among the models, LASSO demonstrates the most balanced and robust performance, achieving one of the highest recall scores on the test set while maintaining comparable AUC and F1-score to other models. In contrast, although tree-based models such as Random Forest and XGBoost achieve slightly higher AUC values, their recall does not consistently outperform LASSO, and their susceptibility to overfitting reduces confidence in their generalisability. As such, LASSO is preferred as the final model due to its strong recall performance, stability across training and test sets, and greater interpretability, making it more suitable for fraud detection in an imbalanced setting.

## Results & Plots

This showcases the plot based on the evaluation metrics \## ROC Curves

```{r plot-roc, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
plot_colors <- c(LR = "#E41A1C", LASSO = "#984EA3", DT = "#377EB8",
                 RF = "#4DAF4A", XGB = "#FF7F00")

plot(results$LR$roc_obj,    col = plot_colors["LR"],    lwd = 2, main = "ROC Curves")
plot(results$LASSO$roc_obj, col = plot_colors["LASSO"], lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
plot(results$DT$roc_obj,    col = plot_colors["DT"],    lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
plot(results$RF$roc_obj,    col = plot_colors["RF"],    lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
plot(results$XGB$roc_obj,   col = plot_colors["XGB"],   lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = c(
         sprintf("LR    (%.3f)", results$LR$auc_roc),
         sprintf("LASSO (%.3f)", results$LASSO$auc_roc),
         sprintf("DT    (%.3f)", results$DT$auc_roc),
         sprintf("RF    (%.3f)", results$RF$auc_roc),
         sprintf("XGB   (%.3f)", results$XGB$auc_roc)
       ),
       col = plot_colors, lwd = 2)
```

### Precision-Recall Curves

```{r plot-pr, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
plot(results$LR$pr_obj,    col = plot_colors["LR"],    lwd = 2,
     main = "Precision-Recall Curves", auc.main = FALSE)
plot(results$LASSO$pr_obj, col = plot_colors["LASSO"], lwd = 2, add = TRUE, auc.main = FALSE)
plot(results$DT$pr_obj,    col = plot_colors["DT"],    lwd = 2, add = TRUE, auc.main = FALSE)
plot(results$RF$pr_obj,    col = plot_colors["RF"],    lwd = 2, add = TRUE, auc.main = FALSE)
plot(results$XGB$pr_obj,   col = plot_colors["XGB"],   lwd = 2, add = TRUE, auc.main = FALSE)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(
         sprintf("LR    (%.3f)", results$LR$auc_pr),
         sprintf("LASSO (%.3f)", results$LASSO$auc_pr),
         sprintf("DT    (%.3f)", results$DT$auc_pr),
         sprintf("RF    (%.3f)", results$RF$auc_pr),
         sprintf("XGB   (%.3f)", results$XGB$auc_pr)
       ),
       col = plot_colors, lwd = 2)
```

### Model Performance Bar Chart

```{r plot-bar, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
plot_df <- melt(summary_tbl, id.vars = "Model",
                measure.vars = c("AUC_ROC", "PR_AUC", "F1"),
                variable.name = "Metric", value.name = "Score")

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Model, y = Score, fill = Metric)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
    width = 0.6
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "AUC_ROC" = "#1F3A5F",   # navy
    "PR_AUC"  = "#4F8A8B",   # teal
    "F1"      = "#FFE600"    # yellow
  )) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Model Performance Comparison (Test Set)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Score",
    fill = "Metric"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5)
  )
```

### Logistic Regression Summary

```{r lr-summary}
summary(fit_lr$finalModel)
```

The logistic regression results are evaluated using a significance threshold of p \< 0.05, with several variables identified as statistically significant contributors to fraud (AAER = 1). These include SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO, REC_TO_SALES, ACCRUALS, ROA, DEBT_TO_EQUITY, LEASE_COMM, NULL_RSST_FLAG, Z_score, and INVENTORY_GROWTH. The direction of the coefficients provides insight into their relationship with fraud risk. Variables such as SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO, LEASE_COMM, and NULL_RSST_FLAG have positive coefficients, indicating that higher values are associated with an increased likelihood of fraud. In particular, LEASE_COMM remains highly significant with a large positive coefficient, reinforcing its importance as a key driver of fraudulent behaviour. Conversely, variables such as REC_TO_SALES, ACCRUALS, ROA, DEBT_TO_EQUITY, Z_score, and INVENTORY_GROWTH have negative coefficients, suggesting that stronger financial health, better earnings quality, and more stable operations are associated with a lower likelihood of fraud.

In terms of magnitude, the coefficient for LEASE_COMM (β = 0.1366) indicates that for every one-unit increase in the transformed LEASE_COMM variable, the log-odds of a firm being classified as fraudulent increase by 0.1366, holding other variables constant. In odds terms, this corresponds to an approximate 14.6% increase in the odds of fraud (e\^0.1366 0.1366 ≈ 1.146), highlighting the strong impact of lease commitments on fraud risk. Overall, these findings are consistent with prior model results, where indicators related to financial stability, earnings quality, and operational irregularities play a crucial role in distinguishing fraudulent firms. While some variables such as INDUSTRY_DIFF, REVENUE_DIFF, and CFO_NI are marginally significant (p \< 0.1), others do not show strong statistical evidence in this specification, with LEASE_COMM and REC_TO_SALES standing out as particularly influential variables.

Conversely, variables such as ACC_REC_GROWTH, SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO, ACCRUALS, CFO_NI, DEBT_TO_ASSETS, M_score, INVENTORY_GROWTH, and FIRM_SIZE are not statistically significant at the 5% level, implying limited explanatory power within this model. Overall, the presence of multiple significant predictors suggests that the model captures meaningful relationships associated with fraudulent reporting. However, statistical significance alone does not determine overall model quality, and it should be interpreted alongside performance metrics such as AUC, recall, and F1-score when assessing the model’s effectiveness.

### LASSO

#### Lambda Selection (CV ROC vs Lambda)

```{r lasso-lambda, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
plot(fit_lasso)
```

#### Coefficient Path

```{r lasso-coef-path, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
plot(fit_lasso$finalModel, xvar = "lambda", label = TRUE)
abline(v = log(fit_lasso$bestTune$lambda), col = "red", lty = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = sprintf("Best lambda = %.5f", fit_lasso$bestTune$lambda),
       col = "red", lty = 2)
```

#### Retained Coefficients at Best Lambda

```{r lasso-coefs}
coef(fit_lasso$finalModel, s = fit_lasso$bestTune$lambda)
```

The LASSO model performs variable selection by shrinking less important coefficients to zero, resulting in a more parsimonious set of predictors. In this model, variables such as levi, net_margin, and margin_jump are shrunk to zero, indicating that they do not contribute significantly to the prediction of fraud (AAER = 1) after regularisation. The remaining variables largely align with the logistic regression results, reinforcing consistency across models. In particular, SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO, LEASE_COMM, NULL_RSST_FLAG, and REVENUE_DIFF have positive coefficients, suggesting that higher values are associated with increased fraud risk, while variables such as REC_TO_SALES, ACCRUALS, ROA, DEBT_TO_EQUITY, Z_score, and INVENTORY_GROWTH are negatively associated with fraud, reflecting stronger financial health and operational stability.

In terms of magnitude, SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO (β ≈ 1.66) and REC_TO_SALES (β ≈ −1.02) exhibit the largest effects in the model, indicating they are among the strongest predictors of fraud. For example, the coefficient for LEASE_COMM (β ≈ 0.1343) implies that a one-unit increase in the transformed variable is associated with an approximate 14.4% increase in the odds of fraud (e\^0.1343 = 1.144), holding other variables constant. Overall, the LASSO results not only reduce model complexity but also highlight a consistent and economically meaningful set of variables that drive fraud risk, further strengthening confidence in the key predictors identified across models.

### Decision Tree

```{r save-dt}
#| results: hide
#Export and resize the pruned decision tree to desired scale
png("decision_tree.png", width = 5000, height = 3000, res = 150)
rpart.plot(fit_dt$finalModel, type = 4, extra = 104,
           main = "Pruned Decision Tree", cex = 0.7)
dev.off()
```

```{r plot-dt}
#| echo = FALSE
knitr::include_graphics("decision_tree.png")
```

The pruned decision tree applies the 1-SE rule to select the simplest model within one standard error of the minimum cross-validation error, resulting in a more interpretable structure while avoiding overfitting. The tree’s splitting behaviour is consistent with the findings from the logistic regression and LASSO models, with LEASE_COMM appearing as the root split (LEASE_COMM \< 8.7). This reinforces its role as the most discriminating variable for fraud classification and aligns with its strong positive coefficient and statistical significance observed in earlier models.

The left branch (LEASE_COMM \< 8.7), which represents the majority of observations (approximately 83% and largely NonFraud), exhibits deeper and more complex splitting patterns. In contrast, the right branch (LEASE_COMM ≥ 8.7) contains a higher concentration of fraud cases and requires fewer conditions to isolate high-risk firms, indicating that elevated lease commitments act as a strong primary trigger for fraud detection.

High-Confidence Fraud Paths (≥ 90%)

The tree identifies 7 terminal nodes where fraud probability exceeds 90%, representing the most reliable fraud signals. These paths are critical as they highlight combinations of variables under which firms are almost certainly classified as fraudulent.

#### Path 1 — Fraud (90%)

LEASE_COMM \< 8.7 → ACCRUALS ≥ -0.24 → margin_jump \< 0.95 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY ≥ -2 → Z_score ≥ 0.53 → ROA \< 5.4 → ROA \< -0.74 → INDUSTRY_DIFF ≥ 0.45 → REVENUE_DIFF \< 0.12

Interpretation: This path captures firms with relatively lower lease commitments but very weak profitability (low ROA) combined with industry-relative anomalies and constrained revenue growth, suggesting potential underperformance masking or manipulation.

#### Path 2 — Fraud (94%)

LEASE_COMM \< 8.7 → ACCRUALS ≥ -0.24 → margin_jump \< 0.95 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY ≥ -2 → Z_score ≥ 0.53 → ROA \< 5.4 → ROA \< -0.74 → INDUSTRY_DIFF ≥ 0.45 → REVENUE_DIFF ≥ 0.12

Interpretation: Similar to Path 1 but with higher revenue growth, which may indicate inconsistent signals between weak profitability and rising revenue, a potential red flag for revenue manipulation.

#### Path 3 — Fraud (100%)

LEASE_COMM \< 8.7 → ACCRUALS ≥ -0.24 → margin_jump \< 0.95 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY ≥ -2 → Z_score ≥ 0.53 → ROA \< 5.4 → ROA ≥ -0.74 → INDUSTRY_DIFF \< 0.45

Interpretation: This path reflects a perfect fraud classification, where firms exhibit a combination of moderate profitability but abnormal positioning relative to industry benchmarks, suggesting structural inconsistencies.

#### Path 4 — Fraud (92%)

LEASE_COMM \< 8.7 → ACCRUALS ≥ -0.24 → margin_jump \< 0.95 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY ≥ -2 → Z_score \< 0.53 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY ≥ 3.6 → LEASE_COMM ≥ 0.96

Interpretation: This highlights firms with weak financial health (low Z-score) and high leverage, even within lower lease commitment groups, indicating financial pressure that may incentivise fraud.

#### Path 5 — Fraud (92%)

LEASE_COMM ≥ 8.7 → REC_TO_SALES ≥ 0.51 → SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO ≥ -0.13 → ACCRUALS ≥ -0.17 → levi ≥ 1.1

Interpretation: In high lease commitment firms, fraud is associated with aggressive revenue recognition (high REC_TO_SALES) and weaker asset quality, combined with leverage — a classic earnings manipulation pattern.

#### Path 6 — Fraud (96%)

LEASE_COMM ≥ 8.7 → ACCRUALS \< -0.16 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY ≥ 6.6

Interpretation: This is one of the strongest signals: firms with extreme accrual behaviour and very high leverage are highly likely to be fraudulent, reflecting significant financial stress and accounting distortion.

#### Path 7 — Fraud (91%)

LEASE_COMM ≥ 8.7 → ACCRUALS \< -0.16 → DEBT_TO_EQUITY \< 6.6 → margin_jump ≥ 0.75

Interpretation: Even when leverage is not extreme, the combination of abnormal accruals and unusually high profit growth signals potential manipulation of earnings performance.

### Random Forest — Variable Importance

```{r plot-rf-imp, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
rf_imp <- varImp(fit_rf)$importance
rf_imp$Feature <- rownames(rf_imp)
rf_imp <- rf_imp[order(-rf_imp$Overall), ][1:15, ]
rf_imp$Overall_pct <- rf_imp$Overall / sum(rf_imp$Overall) * 100

ggplot(rf_imp, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Overall_pct), y = Overall_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4DAF4A") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Overall_pct, 1)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +  # prevent labels being cut off
  labs(title = "Top 15 Features — Random Forest", x = NULL, y = "Importance") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

```

The Random Forest model identifies variable importance based on the mean decrease in node impurity (Gini importance) across all trees in the ensemble, providing a robust, aggregated view of each predictor's contribution to fraud classification. The results reveal a striking dominance of LEASE_COMM, with an importance score of 54.1, more than five times that of the second-ranked variable, ACCRUALS (10.3). This disproportionate gap underscores LEASE_COMM as the single most powerful discriminator of fraudulent firms in the dataset, consistent with its role as the root node split in the decision tree and its high significance and large positive coefficient in both the logistic regression and LASSO models. Its importance across all four model specifications provides particularly strong cross-model validation of its relevance as a fraud indicator.

Beyond LEASE_COMM, the next tier of important variables — ACCRUALS (10.3), REC_TO_SALES (7.5), margin_jump (5.9), DEBT_TO_EQUITY (5.6), and Z_score (4.9), collectively capture earnings quality, leverage, and financial distress dimensions that have consistently appeared as meaningful predictors across models. ROA (2.9) and EMPLOYEE_DIFF (2.6) contribute moderately, with the latter serving as a proxy for operational irregularities such as unusual workforce changes that may accompany fraudulent reporting periods. Variables including net_margin (2.0), SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO (1.6), INVENTORY_GROWTH (0.9), DEBT_TO_ASSETS (0.8), FIRM_SIZE (0.6), ACC_REC_GROWTH (0.3), and M_score (0.2) register lower importance scores, suggesting that while they carry some discriminatory signal, their contribution is marginal once the stronger predictors are accounted for within the ensemble. Notably, SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO, which exhibited among the largest coefficient magnitudes in the LASSO model, ranks lower here, reflecting the difference between marginal predictive contribution in a regularised linear model versus relative impurity reduction across an ensemble of trees.

Overall, the Random Forest importance rankings reinforce a consistent narrative: lease commitments, accruals quality, receivables intensity, and leverage structure are the primary drivers of fraud classification, while variables related to firm size, inventory changes, and composite scores such as M_score play a secondary, supporting role.

### XGBoost — Variable Importance

```{r plot-xgb-imp, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
xgb_imp <- varImp(fit_xgb)$importance
xgb_imp$Feature <- rownames(xgb_imp)
xgb_imp <- xgb_imp[order(-xgb_imp$Overall), ][1:15, ]
xgb_imp$Overall_pct <- xgb_imp$Overall / sum(xgb_imp$Overall) * 100

ggplot(xgb_imp, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Overall_pct), y = Overall_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#FF7F00") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Overall_pct, 1)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 15 Features — XGBoost", x = NULL, y = "Importance") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The XGBoost model measures variable importance based on the gain metric, the average improvement in model accuracy contributed by a variable across all splits where it is used, providing a performance-weighted view of each predictor's contribution. As with the Random Forest, LEASE_COMM dominates with an importance score of 43.4, reaffirming its status as the most discriminating predictor of fraudulent behaviour across all model specifications. While its absolute importance score is lower than in the Random Forest (43.4 versus 54.1), its relative lead over all other variables remains substantial, with ACCRUALS ranking second at 10.6.

The ordering of the top variables in XGBoost closely mirrors that of the Random Forest: ACCRUALS (10.6), REC_TO_SALES (8.3), margin_jump (8.1), DEBT_TO_EQUITY (6.4), and Z_score (4.6), suggesting broad consistency in which variables carry the most predictive signal, regardless of the ensemble method or objective function used. A notable difference is that EMPLOYEE_DIFF ranks higher in XGBoost (3.9) relative to its position in the Random Forest, while SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO drops to the bottom of the top 15 (1.4) in XGBoost compared to its stronger presence in the LASSO. This divergence likely reflects the gradient boosting framework's sequential correction of residuals, which may attribute less marginal gain to SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO once other correlated predictors have already been utilised in earlier boosting rounds.

Variables in the lower tier: ACC_REC_GROWTH (1.6), FIRM_SIZE (1.6), INVENTORY_GROWTH (1.5), REVENUE_DIFF (1.5), DEBT_TO_ASSETS (1.4), and SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO (1.4), contribute modestly, functioning as auxiliary signals rather than primary fraud drivers. The broadly flat importance distribution across this lower tier, contrasted with the sharp drop-off from LEASE_COMM, suggests that the XGBoost model's discrimination power is heavily concentrated in a small core set of variables, with the remaining features providing incremental refinement rather than fundamental classification power.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Final Interpretation and Judgement

### Model Interpretation Strategy and Rationale

Although LASSO is selected as the final model due to its strong recall performance and overall stability, it is not used as the sole basis for interpretation. This is because LASSO primarily performs variable selection through coefficient shrinkage, focusing on the magnitude of coefficients rather than statistical significance, and does not provide clear thresholds or interpretable decision rules for practitioners.

Given that all models (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost) exhibit comparable performance on the test set, it is more robust to adopt a cross-model validation approach for interpretation. Specifically, variables that are consistently identified as important across different model types, including statistical significance (logistic regression), feature importance (tree-based models), and splitting behaviour (decision tree), are more likely to represent true underlying drivers of fraud, rather than model-specific artefacts.

In addition, the inclusion of the decision tree allows us to derive explicit threshold-based rules, which enhances interpretability by showing that when certain variables exceed specific levels, firms are more likely to be classified as fraudulent. This is particularly valuable in a forensic or audit context, where actionable insights and clear decision boundaries are required.

### Common Significant Variables Across Models

By comparing results across logistic regression, decision tree, and ensemble models (Random Forest and XGBoost), a consistent set of variables emerges as key predictors of fraud:

1.  LEASE_COMM
2.  ACCRUALS
3.  REC_TO_SALES
4.  SOFT_ASSETS_RATIO
5.  DEBT_TO_EQUITY
6.  ROA
7.  Z_score

These variables are:

-   Statistically significant in logistic regression (p \< 0.05)
-   Ranked highly in feature importance for Random Forest and XGBoost
-   Frequently used as splitting variables in the decision tree

### Final Interpretation (Economic Meaning)

Finally, we recommend that EY adopts our 4-step KYC screening rule, derived from the decision tree model, to identify firms that may be at elevated risk of fraud.

We focus on the highlighted decision path below, which yields an 88% observed fraud rate and is driven by variables that are both statistically significant and economically meaningful.

```{r plot-dt-chosen-path}
#| echo = FALSE
knitr::include_graphics("decision_tree_chosen_path.png")
```

After reversing the log transformation, firms that satisfy all four conditions below fall into this high-risk segment and should be prioritised for further investigation at an early stage:

1.  Lease Commitments ≥ \~6,000
2.  Accruals ≥ \~ -0.17
3.  Debt-to-Equity ≥ \~735% (≈ 7.35×)
4.  Receivables-to-Sales \< \~67%

### Integrated Interpretation

This rule captures a consistent profile of firms under financial pressure with signs of accounting-driven earnings distortion:

-   High lease commitments indicate substantial fixed obligations, creating pressure to sustain performance and meet contractual payments.
-   High leverage (debt-to-equity ≥ \~735%) reflects an extremely debt-heavy capital structure, increasing financial distress risk and incentivising stronger reported performance.
-   Accruals ≥ \~ -0.17 suggest a greater reliance on accounting adjustments relative to cash flows, pointing to lower earnings quality and potential earnings manipulation.
-   Receivables-to-sales \< \~67% indicates that fraud in this segment is less likely driven by receivables inflation, and more likely through accrual-based or broader accounting manipulation.

In combination, this segment reflects firms that:

-   face significant financial pressure (high leverage and fixed obligations)
-   exhibit abnormal accounting behaviour (divergence between earnings and cash flow)
-   may be incentivised to misrepresent financial performance.

This provides a strong economic rationale for fraudulent behaviour and explains the elevated fraud rate observed in this segment.

Overall, firms exhibiting both high financial pressure and signs of accounting distortion are significantly more likely to be fraudulent, supporting the use of this rule as an effective early-stage screening tool.

## References

Ahlawat, P., O’Niell, C., Mohan, A., & Wiles, T. (2022, October 5). *Solving Software’s R&D Conundrum*. Boston Consulting Group. Retrieved from <https://www.bcg.com/publications/2022/software-companies-tackling-research-and-development-conundrum?>

Altman, E. I., Iwanicz‐Drozdowska, M., Laitinen, E. K., & Suvas, A. (2016). Financial Distress Prediction in an International Context: A Review and Empirical Analysis of Altman's Z‐Score Model. Journal of International Financial Management and Accounting, 28(2), 131-171. <https://doi.org/10.1111/jifm.12053>

Brazel, J. F., Jones, K. L., & Lian, Q. (2024). Auditor Use of Benchmarks to Assess Fraud Risk: The Case for Industry Data. *Journal of Forensic Accounting Research*, *9*(1), 23–57. [https://doiorg.libproxy.smu.edu.sg/10.2308/JFAR-2023-037](https://research-ebsco-com.libproxy.smu.edu.sg/c/wj63el/viewer/pdf/l5finbgid5)
